content:
# Matthew Chapter 21 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 21 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 21:5 — "humble" vs. "gentle" for πραΰς and "daughter of Zion" vs. "people of Jerusalem"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "gentle and mounted upon a donkey... son of a beast-of-burden" | Literal; πραΰς as "gentle"; preserves "daughter of Sion" |
| LOGOS | "humble, and mounted on a donkey... the foal of a beast of burden" | Formal; πραΰς as "humble"; "daughter of Zion" preserved |
| PNEUMA | "humble, riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey" / "people of Jerusalem" | Dynamic; replaces "daughter of Zion" with modern equivalent; replaces "beast of burden" with "donkey" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "humble" (LOGOS) over "gentle" (EMET) — πραΰς in this prophetic context (Zechariah 9:9) connotes humility of station, not gentle temperament. Preserved "daughter of Zion" as a recognized prophetic formula. Retained "beast of burden" per the Hebrew source.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "people of Jerusalem" loses the OT prophetic register.

---

## 21:10 — "was shaken" vs. "was stirred up" vs. "buzzed with excitement" for ἐσείσθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all the city was shaken" | Literal rendering of σείω (seismic language) |
| LOGOS | "the whole city was stirred up" | Formal equivalent, softening the seismic metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "the entire city buzzed with excitement" | Dynamic rendering losing the earthquake resonance entirely |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "was stirred up" (LOGOS) for readability, but notes EMET's observation as critically important. The verb σείω is the same root used for the earthquake at the crucifixion (27:51) and resurrection (28:2). Matthew is using seismic language deliberately. SOPHIA's rendering captures the disturbance without the awkwardness of "shaken" applied to a city, while PNEUMA's "buzzed with excitement" loses the ominous undertone completely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS, with EMET's literal insight preserved in council notes.

---

## 21:12 — "pigeons" vs. "doves" for περιστεράς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] selling the doves" | Literal — περιστερά is "dove" |
| LOGOS | "those who sold pigeons" | Formal — "pigeons" used as more accessible English term for the sacrificial bird |
| PNEUMA | "those selling doves" | Dynamic — retains "doves" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "pigeons" (LOGOS). Though περιστερά covers both doves and pigeons (same family), in the temple context these were the affordable sacrificial birds prescribed for the poor (Leviticus 5:7). "Pigeons" better conveys the humble, commercial nature of the trade. However, this is a minor point.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Marginal difference.

---

## 21:13 — "den of robbers" vs. "cave of robbers" vs. "hideout for thieves"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a cave of robbers" | Literal rendering of σπήλαιον λῃστῶν |
| LOGOS | "a den of robbers" | Traditional formal English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a hideout for thieves" | Dynamic modernization |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "den of robbers" (LOGOS). This is the established English rendering of Jeremiah 7:11, and preserving it maintains the intertextual connection. PNEUMA's "hideout for thieves" is clear but breaks the scriptural echo. EMET's "cave" is more literal (σπήλαιον = cave) but "den" is the recognized biblical English term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional phrasing preserves the Jeremiah allusion.

---

## 21:19 — "May no fruit ever come from you again" vs. "May you never bear fruit again"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "No longer from you may fruit come into the age" | Ultra-literal with "into the age" for εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα |
| LOGOS | "May no fruit ever come from you again" | Formal English rendering of the curse |
| PNEUMA | "May you never bear fruit again!" | Dynamic, slightly more colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. EMET's "into the age" is wooden in English. Both LOGOS and PNEUMA capture the finality of the curse; LOGOS is slightly more formal and closer to the Greek structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor stylistic preference.

---

## 21:28 — "sons" vs. "children" for τέκνα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "A man had two children... 'Child, go today, work in the vineyard'" | Literal rendering of τέκνα/τέκνον |
| LOGOS | "A man had two sons... 'Son, go and work in the vineyard today'" | Formal rendering using "sons" for contextual clarity |
| PNEUMA | "A man had two sons... 'Son, go work in the vineyard today'" | Dynamic, same choice as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "sons" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Greek τέκνον is gender-neutral ("child"), but the parable's context makes clear these are sons. LOGOS/PNEUMA's rendering removes unnecessary ambiguity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's literal "children" is noted.

---

## 21:29 — "changed his mind" vs. "having regretted" vs. "changed his mind"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having regretted, he went" | Literal rendering of μεταμεληθείς |
| LOGOS | "he changed his mind and went" | Renders the verb as cognitive change |
| PNEUMA | "he changed his mind and went" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "changed his mind" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The verb μεταμέλομαι denotes a change of attitude or feeling — "changed his mind" captures this clearly. EMET's "regretted" implies emotional remorse that goes beyond the parable's simple narrative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 21:30 — "I go, sir" vs. "'Sure, Dad'"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "'I [go], lord'" | Literal, with κύριε rendered as "lord" |
| LOGOS | "'I go, sir'" | Formal English, with κύριε as polite address |
| PNEUMA | "'Sure, Dad'" | Dynamic, colloquial modernization |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "I go, sir" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "Sure, Dad" is vivid but anachronistic and alters the register of the parable. The second son's response should carry a note of formal compliance — the contrast between his polite words and his disobedience is the point.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's rendering is too colloquial for this context.

---

## 21:33 — "tenants" vs. "farmers" for γεωργοῖς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "farmers" | Literal — γεωργός = farmer/cultivator |
| LOGOS | "tenants" | Formal — emphasizes the leasing relationship |
| PNEUMA | "tenant farmers" | Dynamic — combines both meanings |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "tenants" (LOGOS). The parable's point depends on the tenants' obligation to the owner. "Farmers" (EMET) describes their occupation but not the crucial relationship of accountability. PNEUMA's "tenant farmers" is accurate but slightly unwieldy.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The relational dynamic is central to the parable.

---

## 21:41 — "He will put those wretches to a miserable death" vs. dynamic paraphrase

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The evil [ones] he will evilly destroy" | Preserves the Greek wordplay (κακοὺς κακῶς) |
| LOGOS | "He will put those wretches to a miserable death" | Formal English capturing the severity |
| PNEUMA | "He'll bring those wretches to a wretched end!" | Dynamic rendering attempting to preserve the wordplay |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. EMET captures the Greek paronomasia (κακοὺς κακῶς — "the evil ones evilly") but it reads awkwardly in English. PNEUMA's "wretches to a wretched end" cleverly echoes the Greek wordplay. This is one case where PNEUMA's dynamic rendering is noteworthy, but SOPHIA follows LOGOS for consistency of register.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's wordplay is acknowledged as effective.

---

## 21:42 — "cornerstone" vs. "head of the corner"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the head of [the] corner" | Literal rendering of κεφαλὴν γωνίας |
| LOGOS | "the cornerstone" | Standard English architectural term |
| PNEUMA | "the cornerstone" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "cornerstone" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Greek phrase κεφαλὴν γωνίας is an idiom for the key structural stone. "Cornerstone" is the established English rendering that captures the architectural and christological meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase vs. formal rendering | 6 | Resolved in favor of formal rendering (LOGOS) |
| Register/tone differences (colloquial vs. formal) | 2 | Formal register preserved |
| OT prophetic language preservation | 2 | Prophetic formulas retained |
| Greek wordplay/idiom translation | 2 | Meaning-based rendering preferred |
| Vocabulary precision (tenants vs. farmers, etc.) | 2 | Relational/contextual meaning prioritized |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 21 continues the pattern from chapter 20: LOGOS and SOPHIA show the closest alignment. The key divergence axis remains PNEUMA's modernizing and colloquializing tendency. In this chapter, PNEUMA's dynamic rendering of the seismic language in v.10 ("buzzed with excitement") represents the most significant loss of meaning, as it eliminates a structural literary connection that Matthew builds across the crucifixion and resurrection accounts. EMET's literal precision is especially valuable for the wordplay in v.41 and the seismic language in v.10.

---

*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, Bible Translation Project*
